mappings.rationaleAI: The Pacific Ocean as the largest oceanic division hosting marine communities (B.2) and the Notogaean realm including tropical Pacific islands (D.2).

Ecology as the discipline studying biogeography, biomes, food webs, and organism-environment relationships across terrestrial (A) and aquatic (B) ecosystems, distribution of organisms (C), and biogeographic regions (D).
The Arctic as a specialized polar biome (A.1.d) within the Holarctic biogeographic region (D.1.a).
Grasslands as a major terrestrial biome covering 31-69% of Earth's land area (A.1.c).
The Arctic Ocean as the smallest oceanic division with unique sea-ice ecology and marine communities (B.2).
The Amazon rainforest as the world's largest tropical moist broadleaf forest biome (A.1.c) within the Neogaean realm (D.3).
The Mediterranean Sea as a boundary ecosystem between waters (B.4) within the Holarctic biogeographic region (D.1.a).
Lakes as lacustrine standing-water communities in freshwater inland water ecosystems (B.3.a).
The Great Lakes as a major lacustrine freshwater community (B.3.a) within the Holarctic region of North America (D.1.a).
Ecosystems as the organizational framework for both terrestrial (A) and aquatic (B) biogeographic distributions.
Soil as a limiting factor for living on land (A.1.b) and soil organisms as a major life-form classification by habitat (A.2.b).
The sea as the aquatic environment (B.1) hosting oceanic communities including benthos, plankton, and nekton (B.2).
Forests as major terrestrial biomes including coniferous forest, middle-latitude forest, and tropical rain forest (A.1.c).
Water as a limiting factor for terrestrial life (A.1.b) and as the medium for the aquatic environment (B.1).
Algae as principal primary producers in aquatic ecosystems contributing to oceanic plankton populations (B.2.b) and productivity in aquatic ecosystems (B.5).
Trees as rooted plants classified by habitat within major terrestrial life-forms (A.2.b).
Rain as the dominant freshwater delivery mechanism and a limiting factor for terrestrial life (A.1.b).
Land as the medium for terrestrial life (A.1.a) including all continents and their biomes (A.1).
Deserts as a major terrestrial biome defined by scarce precipitation (A.1.c).
The Caspian Sea as the world's largest inland water body, a lacustrine standing-water community (B.3.a).
Rivers as riverine flowing-water communities including aquatic food webs (B.3.b).
Biodiversity as the variability of life distributed across biogeographic regions (D) and governing the distribution of organisms (C).
Organisms as the individual functional units whose biogeographic distribution is the subject of section C.
Archaea inhabiting extreme environments and ubiquitous habitats as soil organisms and microbial life-forms (A.2.b).
The Yangtze as a major riverine flowing-water community (B.3.b) within the Oriental biogeographic region (D.1.c).
The Indian Ocean hosting diverse marine ecosystems (B.2) bordering the Ethiopian biogeographic region (D.1.b).
The Atlantic Ocean as a major oceanic division hosting marine communities and fisheries (B.2).
The Andes featuring altitudinal climate and ecosystem zonation across biomes (A.1.c) within the Neogaean realm of South America (D.3).
The Sahara as Earth's largest hot desert biome (A.1.c) within the Ethiopian biogeographic region of North Africa (D.1.b).
Polynesia as a Pacific subregion within the Notogaean realm including tropical Pacific islands (D.2).
The Neolithic transition to agriculture fundamentally altered terrestrial biome distribution through land conversion from natural ecosystems to cropland (A.1.c).
